purpose: Freeze the current semantic contract before shared HIR work. M1 must preserve resolved entries; intentional changes require a later M2 decision revision.

status_policy.resolved: The current behavior and the migration contract are explicit.
status_policy.open: A question remains unresolved; the entry names the milestone and condition that close it.
status_policy.deferred: The question is intentionally postponed and must not be treated as an implicit HIR decision.

entries.question: Where does the semantic boundary between native calls, IR operations, bytecode, and the Rust VM live?
entries.decision: Static native and builtin recognition belongs to the semantic checker. IR lowering emits the dedicated len operation for the legacy len path and NativeCall for registered stdlib/member helpers. Bytecode lowering preserves that distinction, and the Rust VM owns dynamic argument validation, mutation, callback invocation, and runtime errors.
entries.owner: TypeChecker, IRCompiler, BytecodeCompiler, and vm-rs runtime
entries.rationale: The current pipeline already has one explicit native_call path and separate legacy len behavior; a shared semantic layer must not make each backend rediscover helper identity or move runtime validation into static analysis.
entries.compatibility_consequences: New stdlib helpers should use the existing native_call boundary. Adding an opcode or changing native ABI requires an explicit M0.5B/M4 decision and C++/artifact/Rust parity evidence.

entries.question: How are source locations and source text carried into emitted artifacts and runtime diagnostics?
entries.decision: Artifact debug_sources and debug_locations are optional additive cdbc sections. Source references inside an artifact are local zero-based indexes; source line and column values are one-based and positive. Locations are sparse, and the Rust VM uses them for source-aware runtime errors and call stacks. Snapshot-local HIR identities are not persistent artifact keys.
entries.owner: SourceMap, BytecodeTextEmitter, Rust artifact parser, and Rust VM diagnostics
entries.rationale: The current artifact contract distinguishes executable core data from optional debug metadata and already rejects invalid source/function/instruction references.
entries.compatibility_consequences: HIR migration must preserve existing one-based diagnostic coordinates and optional artifact metadata. Persistent cross-build source identities remain a later M4B concern.

entries.question: What identifies a binding and how are lexical scopes resolved?
entries.decision: Bindings are resolved lexically at compile time to the nearest declaration in the active scope. Blocks create scopes, same-scope duplicate declarations are rejected, and unresolved reads or assignments are type errors before IR generation. Lowering uses compiler-generated resolved names rather than runtime scope lookup.
entries.rationale: Compile-time resolution makes shadowing and undefined-name behavior deterministic and removes runtime scope machinery from the current backend.
affected_consumers: M1B declaration collection and name resolution
entries.compatibility_consequences: M1 may replace string/pointer lookup incrementally, but resolved target identity and current shadowing/diagnostic behavior must remain equivalent during migration.

entries.question: How do closures capture and mutate bindings, and do loop targets cross function boundaries?
entries.decision: Closures capture enclosing local cells by reference. Assignment updates the shared cell, so all closures observing that binding see the new value. A nested function has its own function/loop context: break and continue in a nested function do not target an enclosing lexical loop.
entries.owner: TypeChecker loop/function contexts and C++/Rust runtime cells
entries.rationale: Shared cells are the current runtime identity for mutable captured locals; lexical function boundaries are required to avoid compiling control-flow jumps into the wrong function.
affected_consumers: Rust VM call frames and cells
entries.compatibility_consequences: HIR capture sets and control-flow targets must lower to the existing shared-cell and function-frame behavior without executing old and new programs twice.

entries.question: Which phase owns a diagnostic and when does compilation stop or recover?
entries.decision: Lexer, parser, type, and import diagnostics are front-end phase errors. Parser recovery may collect multiple statement-boundary parse errors; lexer, import, and type checking stop after the first reported error. Compile and runtime diagnostics are separate categories and are locationless unless a future change explicitly supplies source context or artifact debug metadata.
entries.owner: Lexer, Parser, FrontendSession, TypeChecker, CLI, and Rust VM
entries.rationale: Keeping stage ownership explicit prevents HIR or backend failures from being misreported as parse/type errors and preserves the existing recovery contract.
entries.compatibility_consequences: A structural HIR migration must preserve diagnostic kind, first-error/recovery behavior, file context, source snippet, caret, and exit status for the bound error corpus.

entries.question: What is the evaluation order for expressions, calls, collections, assignments, and logical operators?
entries.decision: Expression children are evaluated in source order. Binary operands, array/map/struct elements, call arguments, and member-call receiver/arguments are left-to-right. Logical && and || evaluate the left operand first and conditionally evaluate the right operand. Current assignment lowering evaluates a variable RHS, or evaluates index/field target components before the assigned value, exactly as represented by the existing IR lowering.
entries.owner: Parser precedence, IRCompiler expression lowering, and VM instruction execution
entries.rationale: Side effects in mutable collections, callbacks, assignments, and runtime errors make ordering observable; HIR cannot treat this as an optimization detail.
affected_consumers: M1C typed expressions and assignment targets
entries.compatibility_consequences: M1 shadow comparisons must compare IR/artifact ordering for side-effecting cases; any intentional reordering is a semantic revision, not a HIR cleanup.

entries.question: Which runtime values are shared and how do mutation and errors propagate?
entries.decision: Arrays, maps, and structs are mutable reference values; aliases observe in-place updates. Closure cells are shared mutable references. Array helper callbacks use the documented snapshot/short-circuit rules. A runtime error aborts the current execution and propagates through the C++ or Rust VM call boundary with no implicit recovery.
entries.owner: Value/runtime implementations and Rust VM execution
entries.rationale: Reference identity and callback snapshot behavior affect observable output and cannot be reconstructed from static types alone.
affected_consumers: M1C collection and field semantic nodes
entries.compatibility_consequences: Typed representations may carry ownership/reference facts, but lowering must preserve aliases, snapshot boundaries, short-circuiting, and runtime error text.

entries.question: What is a module identity and how do imports, cycles, visibility, and re-exports behave?
entries.decision: A source module is identified by its canonical resolved path; direct CLI roots are grouped in command-line order. Imports resolve relative to the importing file and then configured search paths. Duplicate canonical imports are no-ops, import cycles are rejected, imported top-level declarations are module-private unless exported, namespace aliases do not pollute the importer, and re-exports forward selected public names without making them local declarations.
entries.rationale: The current frontend already exposes module metadata to later checking, so HIR must preserve identity and visibility before separate compilation is designed.
affected_consumers: M1B declaration collection and imported symbols
entries.compatibility_consequences: M1 may materialize module/symbol IDs but must preserve canonical path resolution, cycle diagnostics, export visibility, and namespace behavior.

entries.question: Are emitted module interfaces already the compilation input for importers?
entries.decision: No. Module-interface output is deterministic public API metadata for inspection, while current import checking and lowering still consume loaded dependency AST bodies in one frontend session. Separate compilation, interface ownership, cache keys, and whether interfaces become linkable inputs are deferred.
entries.rationale: Changing this boundary would be a separate-compilation architecture decision and is outside a behavior-preserving HIR migration.
entries.compatibility_consequences: M1 must not silently treat module-interface text as a linker or type-checking artifact. Any interface-driven importer requires a new decision and migration comparison.

closure.condition: Choose interface ownership, separate-compilation input shape, cache invalidation keys, and compatibility behavior for stale/missing interfaces.

entries.question: How are pattern bindings, guards, exhaustiveness, reachability, and nullable cases interpreted?
entries.decision: Patterns bind names in an arm-local scope. Guards are evaluated after pattern bindings and do not contribute to exhaustiveness. Enum variants, named-struct records, literals, nullable values, and boolean domains use the current coverage rules; open number/string domains require a wildcard or binding. Unreachable or non-exhaustive arms remain semantic diagnostics.
entries.owner: TypeChecker pattern analysis and IRCompiler match lowering
entries.rationale: Coverage and arm-local scope are semantic facts, while lowering only implements the selected control-flow shape; duplicating coverage decisions in lowering would be unsafe.
affected_consumers: M1D control-flow edges and arm bindings
entries.compatibility_consequences: HIR must carry checked coverage/reachability and binding metadata; IR lowering must not re-infer whether a match is exhaustive.

entries.question: How do OR-pattern alternatives share bindings?
entries.decision: Every OR alternative must bind exactly the same names with mutually compatible types. A successful alternative exposes one shared arm-local binding set; coverage is the union of alternatives and guards remain non-covering.
entries.rationale: A single arm body cannot safely depend on a binding that exists only in some alternatives or has incompatible types.
entries.compatibility_consequences: The semantic model must represent one merged binding per name, not separate backend-only alternatives.

entries.question: What owns source text and coordinates, and what range convention will the shared semantic model use?
entries.decision: A source file owns its display path and original text. Current diagnostics and AST spans preserve source index plus one-based line/column points. M1A1 will add snapshot-stable source identities and complete half-open byte ranges [start, end), while retaining one-based line/column conversion for user-facing output; snapshot-local identities are never persistent cache or artifact keys.
entries.owner: SourceMap/diagnostic layer, with M1A1 source foundation
entries.rationale: HIR, diagnostics, and later tools need one range convention, but existing CLI output must remain line/column compatible during migration.
affected_consumers: M1A1 snapshot identities and source ranges
entries.compatibility_consequences: Existing point diagnostics remain stable; new ranges are derived from original bytes and must be validated for ordering and source ownership.

entries.question: Is a lossless token/trivia representation already part of the compiler semantic contract?
entries.decision: No. The current lexer skips whitespace and line comments and the parser/compiler operate on semantic tokens and AST spans. A lossless token/trivia view is deferred to M1A2; it must be additive and must not create a second lexer or change compiler tokenization.
entries.rationale: Formatter/editor preservation is important but is not required for the first declaration or typed-expression HIR slices.
entries.compatibility_consequences: M1A1 and M1B cannot assume comments are available from the current token stream; any trivia view must prove byte-for-byte reconstruction separately.

closure.condition: Define trivia ownership, exact reconstruction, token/range ordering, and comment retention tests without changing parser semantics.

entries.question: What is type identity, and how are unknown/dynamic values represented?
entries.decision: Primitive types are identified by kind. Named structs and enums are nominal by declaration name; generic arguments are compile-time metadata and named generic structs/enums are invariant. Arrays, maps, nullable types, and function types carry structural nested type information when known. Unknown types are permissive during static checking and defer applicable validation to runtime; they are not a coercion to every known type.
entries.rationale: The semantic model needs one canonical type identity before declaration collection and typed expression nodes can stop re-inferencing types.
entries.compatibility_consequences: HIR type references must preserve nominal names, generic substitution, nullable structure, function signatures, and the distinction between unknown and known types.

entries.question: What assignability and nullable-narrowing rules are fixed for M1?
entries.decision: A value of T is assignable to T?, nil is assignable to T?, and T? is not assignable to non-nullable T except inside the supported direct simple-variable nil-check branches. Known function signatures, collection element types, struct fields, and enum payloads are checked recursively; unknown values retain runtime checks. Narrowing does not implicitly extend to fields, indexes, loops, or post-branch flow.
entries.rationale: This is the current conservative flow contract; broadening it is a coherent M2 dataflow change rather than an incidental HIR refactor.
entries.compatibility_consequences: M1 must materialize existing flow facts and assignability results without adding new narrowing sites. New flow behavior requires a decision revision.

entries.question: How are generic substitution, function compatibility, and variance handled?
entries.decision: Generic parameters are inferred from known argument/expected shapes or supplied explicitly, then erased before runtime lowering. Concrete bounds are checked for inferred and explicit arguments. Function compatibility checks arity, parameter types, and return type recursively. Named generic struct and enum arguments are invariant; unresolved generic callback parameters are static errors when inference cannot bind them.
entries.rationale: Generic metadata must be available to HIR consumers while remaining absent from the current erased runtime representation.
affected_consumers: M1C calls and contextual function values
entries.compatibility_consequences: HIR may retain substituted types and bounds, but bytecode/value layout stays erased until an explicit ABI decision changes it.

duplicated_decision_inventory.canonicalization: Read UTF-8 source lines in manifest order; sort matches by file, line, and pattern; collapse internal whitespace in recorded text; hash the canonical match tuples with SHA-256.
duplicated_decision_inventory.comparison_statistic: Exact category match counts plus canonical match digest; this is a source-site inventory, not a claim that every match is an independent semantic rule.

categories.description: AST-specific lowering and opcode mapping sites that must eventually consume resolved typed nodes.
Binding lookup/recording and resolved-name consumption shared across checking and lowering boundaries.
AST type checking, assignability, statement, pattern, and builtin semantic dispatch sites.

decision_families.deletion_condition: IRCompiler has zero production AST type/name re-inference for migrated expressions and consumes typed semantic nodes.
All migrated reads, assignments, calls, and declarations use snapshot-stable resolved targets with no legacy lookup fallback.
The shared semantic representation owns operation selection and bytecode mapping has no duplicate AST interpretation.
